Once we cross the infinite-period bifurcation ω 1 + ω 2 = 1 line, the limit cycles are broken by fixed points and the oscillations stop. 8.6.3 This problem is actually quite common and there are multiple proofs. Here is one that doesn’t require much prerequisite knowledge. Without loss of generality, assume that q = (0,0), as we can translate to any point and ω   =   ω 1 ω 2   <   1 since exchanging ω 1 with ω 2 is an equivalent problem. Also, we only need to prove that the irrational orbit is dense in {( θ 1 , θ 2 ) : θ 2 = 0} (the bottom of the phase space) since a dense set at a cross section of the torus will also be dense everywhere when translated by the irrational flow. So now we have a somewhat simpler problem to work on. We’ll be using Dirichlet’s approximation theorem, which implies that for every irrational number ω there exist integers a and b such that | ω   −   a b |   <   1 b 2 The orbits that start at ( θ 1 , θ 2 ) = (0,0) with slope a b intersect the bottom of the phase space at { 0 ,   1 b ,   2 b , ⋯   b   −   1 b } So the rational orbit passes within distance 1 2 b of every point at the bottom of the phase space. Now applying the implication of Dirichlet’s approximation theorem n   | ω   −   a b |   <   n b 2   <   1 b   n   =   0 , 1 , 2 , … ( b   −   1 ) As long as 0 ≤ n ≤ b < – 1, the nth intersection of the irrational orbit is within 1 b distance of n b and therefore the first b – 1 iterations of the irrational orbit are within distance 1 b of every point at the bottom of the phase space. Hence the irrational orbit can be made arbitrarily close to any point at the bottom of the phase space, and consequently any point on the entire torus, by picking a large enough value for b . 8.6.5 8.6.7 m r ¨   =   h 2 m r 3   −   k   θ ˙   =   h m r 2   m , k , h   >   0 a) r ( t )   =   r 0   ⇒   r ¨   =   0 m r ¨   =   h 2 m r 3   −   k   →   0   =   h 2 m r 0 3   −   k   ⇒   r 0   =   ( h 2 m k ) 1 3 θ ˙   =   h m r 0 2   =   h m   ( m k h 2 ) 2 3   =   ( k 2 m h ) 1 3   =   ω 0 b) First, we’ll transform the r ¨ equation to a system and then linearize about ( r ,   r ˙ )   =   ( r 0 ,   0 ) . x   =   r       y   =   r ˙   x ˙   =   y       y ˙   =   h 2 m 2 x 3   −   k m A   =   ( 0 1 − 3 h 2 m 2 x 4 0 )   A ( r 0 , 0 )   =   ( 0 1 − 3 h 2 m 2 x 4 0 ) Then the frequency of small radial oscillations is ω r   =   3 h 2 m 2 r 0 4   =   3   h 3 r 0 2   =   3 ω θ c) The winding number ω r ω θ   =   ω θ ω θ   =   3 is irrational and hence the small radial oscillations are quasiperiodic. d) The linearization from part (c) predicts a center, but we didn’t actually check that it’s a center. It really is a center and we can find a conserved quantity; or, if we look closely, we see that the r equation is a nonlinear spring with no damping and no r = r 0 rest position. The solution to r ( t ) is periodic for any initial conditions. The periodicity of r ( t ) implies that θ ˙ is periodic. The θ ˙ equation is also strictly positive. Both these facts together imply that θ ( t ) has a constant finite period, which is determined by the r ( t ) initial conditions. The period can vary for different r ( t ) initial conditions, but the period is constant for each choice of r ( t ) initial conditions. More precisely, ( r ( θ
